questions.question: Let $P\left( n \right)$ be a statement and let $P\left( n \right) \Rightarrow P\left( {n + 1} \right)$ for all natural numbers n, then what will the nature of $P\left( n \right)$ ?
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}P\left( n \right){/tex} be a statement and let {tex}P\left( n \right) \Rightarrow P\left( {n + 1} \right){/tex} for all natural numbers n, then what will the nature of  {tex}P\left( n \right){/tex} ?

options.label: B
options.text: It is true for all n > m, m being a fixed positive integer

questions.explanation: Since the statement is conditional statement ( if....then).

questions.question: Let P(n) b e a statement 2^n < n!, where n is a natural number, then P(n) is true for
questions.question_raw: Let P(n) b e a statement 2n < n!, where n is a natural number, then P(n) is true for
questions.explanation: Since for example n = 4 will give LHS as 16 and RHS as 4! = 1 $\times$ 2 $\times$ 3 $\times$ 4 = 24

questions.question: If x > -1, then the statement (1 + x)^n > 1 + nx is true for
questions.question_raw: If x > -1, then the statement (1 + x)n > 1 + nx is true for
questions.explanation: For example, if x = 0 and n > 1, say n = 2 then the given inequality will become 1^2 > 1, which is not true. Hence both the conditions on x and n are needed.

questions.question: The smallest +ve integer n, for which n! < $\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^n$ holds is
questions.question_raw: The smallest +ve integer n, for which n! < {tex}\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^n{/tex} holds is
questions.explanation:
When n = 2, LHS: n! = 2! = 2 $\times$ 1 = 2
RHS: $(\frac{3}{2})^2= \frac{9}{4}$ 
But if n = 1, the inequation does not hold good.

questions.question: If P(n) = 2 + 4 + 6 +………………..+ 2n, n $ \in $ N, then P(k) = k(k + 1) + 2 $ \Rightarrow $ P (k + 1) = (k + 1) (k + 2) + 2 for all k $ \in $ N. So we can conclude that P(n) = n (n + 1) + 2 for

questions.explanation: Because the statement is incomplete without the conclusion.

questions.question: (x^n - y^n) is divisible by (x - y) for
questions.question_raw: (xn - yn) is divisible by (x - y) for
questions.explanation: Replacing n by 1, 2, 3... we get the expression with the factor (x - y). Hence it is divisible by (x - y).

questions.question: The greatest positive integer, which divides n (n + 1) (n + 2) (n + 3) for all n $ \in $ N , is
questions.question_raw: The greatest positive integer, which divides n (n + 1) (n + 2) (n + 3) for all n {tex} \in {/tex} N , is
questions.explanation: If n = 1 then the statement becomes 1 $\times$ 2 $\times$ 3 $\times$ 4 = 24: the consecutive natural numbers when substituted will be multiples of 24.

questions.question: Let P(n) denote the statement n^2 + n is odd, It is seen that P(n) $ \Rightarrow$ P(n + 1), therefore P(n) is true for all
questions.question_raw: Let P(n) denote the statement n2 + n is odd, It is seen that P(n) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} P(n + 1), therefore P(n) is true for all
questions.explanation: Since if n = 1 the answer is 2 which is even. Also p(1) = p(2) is also not true since p(2) is 6 which is even.

questions.question: The greatest positive integer, which divides (n + 1)(n + 2)(n + 3) ......(n + r) $\forall n \in W$, is
questions.question_raw: The greatest positive integer, which divides (n + 1)(n + 2)(n + 3) ......(n + r) {tex}\forall n \in W{/tex}, is
questions.explanation: If n = 0 the given expression becomes 1 $\times$ 2 $\times$ 3 $\times$ 4........r = r!. Also when n = 1 one more extra term will be there in the product 2 $\times$ 3 $\times$ 4........(r + 1) which is also divisible by r!.

questions.question: The statement P(n) : 1$\times$1! + 2$\times$2! + 3$\times$3! + .....+ n$\times$n! = (n + 1)! - 1 is

questions.explanation: Replace n = 1, 2, 3 then LHS = RHS

questions.question: A student was asked to prove a statement P(n) by method of induction. He proved that P(3) is true and that P(n) $ \Rightarrow $ P(n + 1) for all natural numbers n. On the basis of this the criteria applied on n for P(n) to be true
questions.question_raw: A student was asked to prove a statement P(n) by method of induction. He proved that P(3) is true and that P(n) {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} P(n + 1) for all natural numbers n. On the basis of this the criteria applied on n for  P(n) to be true
questions.explanation: Since by the principle of mathematical induction if p(n) is true for n = 3 then that will be the minimum point to be considered for the process. Also that P(n) $\Rightarrow$ P(n + 1).

questions.question: 3^2n+2 - 8^n - 9 is divisible by 64 for all
questions.question_raw: 32n+2 - 8n - 9 is divisible by 64 for all
questions.explanation: When n = 1 the value is 64. By induction process the consecutive replacement of n = 2, 3, 4.... will be multiples of 64.

questions.question: The inequality n! > 2^n-1 is true
questions.question_raw: The inequality n! > 2n-1 is true
questions.explanation: Since when n = 1, we get the inequality as 1 > 1, which is not true. Also for n = 2, we get 2 > 2, which is false. Hence the given statement is true for n > 2.

questions.question: The statement 3^n > 4n is true for all
questions.question_raw: The statement 3n > 4n is true for all
questions.explanation: 3^n > 4n for all n > 1 can be proved by induction.

questions.question: n^2 + 3n is always divisible by which number, provided n is an integer?
questions.question_raw: n2 + 3n is always divisible by which number, provided n is an integer?
questions.explanation:
P(n) = n^2 + 3n
P(1) = 1 + 3
P(1) = 4
Let’s assume that P(k) is true and divisible by 4. Therefore, P(k) = k^2 + 3k can be written as 4c.
We need to check if P(k + 1) is divisible by 4
P(k+1) = (k + 1)^2 + 3(k + 1)
P(k+1) = k^2 + 1 + 2k + 3k + 3
P(k+1) = k^2 + 5k + 4
P(k+1) = (k^2 + 3k) + 2k + 4
P(k+1) = 4c + 2k + 4
P(k+1) = 4c + 2(k + 2)
Clearly the second part of the equation is not divisible by 4. However P(k) = 4c is divisible by 2 and
P(k + 1) is also divisible by 2. Therefore, 2 divides P(n).

questions.question: Let P(k) = 1 + 3 + 5 + …………….+ (2k - 1) = (3 + k^2). Then which of the following is true?
questions.question_raw: Let P(k) = 1 + 3 + 5 + …………….+ (2k - 1) = (3 + k2). Then which of the following is true?
questions.explanation:
P(k) = 1 + 3 + ... + (2k - 1) = 3 + k^2 .....(i)
When k = 1, LHS of p(k) $\ne$ R.H.S of P(k)
So P(1) is not ture.
Now P(k + 1); 1 + 3 + 5 + .... + (2k - 1) + (2k + 1)
= 3 + (k + 1)^2 .....(ii)
Let P(k) is true 
$\therefore$ 1 + 3 + 5 + ..... + (2k - 1) = k^2 + 3
$\Rightarrow$ 1 + 3 + 5 + .... + (2k - 1)
= 3 + k^2 + 2k + 1 = (k + 1)^2 + 3
$\Rightarrow$ P(k + 1) true 
$\therefore$ P(k) $\Rightarrow$ P(k + 1)

questions.question: Let that P(n) $\Rightarrow$ P(n + 1) for all natural numbers n. Also, if P (m) is true, m $\in $ N, then we conclude that
questions.question_raw: Let that P(n) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(n + 1) for all natural numbers n.  Also, if P (m) is true, m {tex}\in {/tex} N, then we conclude that

options.label: A
options.text: P ( n ) is true for all n

options.label: B
options.text: P (n) is true for all n $ ≥$ m

options.label: C
options.text: P ( n ) is true for all n $<$ m

options.label: D
options.text: P ( n ) is true for all n > m

correct_option_texts: P (n) is true for all n $ ≥$ m
questions.explanation: This criteria is from the basic principle of mathematical induction.

questions.question: Consider the statement P(n) : n^2 $ \ge$ 100. Here P(n) $\Rightarrow$ P(n + 1) for all natural numbers n. What does it mean

options.text: P(n) is true for all n $≥$ 3

options.label: C
options.text: p(n) is true for all n

options.label: D
options.text: P (n) is true for all n $≥ $ 2

questions.explanation: Since the statement is not true for n = 1, we cannot predict the validity for all natural numbers n.

questions.question: If x^n - 1 is divisible by x – k for all n belongs to natural numbers N, then the least positive integral value of k is:
questions.question_raw: If xn - 1 is divisible by x – k for all n belongs to natural numbers N, then the least positive integral value of k is:
questions.explanation:
If f(x) = x^n - 1 is divisible by x - k
Then f(k) = 0
Therefore, k^n = 1 and thus least positive integral value of k is 1.

questions.question: A student was asked to prove a statement P ( n ) by method of induction . He proved P ( k + 1 ) is true whenever P ( k ) Is true for all k ≥ 5 , $k \in N$ and P ( 5 ) is true . On the basis of this he could conclude that P ( n ) is true
questions.question_raw: A student was asked to prove a statement P ( n ) by method of induction . He proved P ( k + 1 ) is true whenever P ( k ) Is true for all k ≥ 5 , {tex}k \in N{/tex} and P ( 5 ) is true . On the basis of this he could conclude that P ( n ) is true
questions.explanation: Yes because if it is true for n = k then it is true for all n ,which is the basic concept of mathematical induction.

questions.question: The sum of the terms in the n^th bracket of the series 1 + (2 + 3 + 4) + (5 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 9)…. is
questions.question_raw: The sum of the terms in the nth bracket of the series 1 + (2 + 3 + 4) + (5 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 9)…. is
questions.explanation:
For n = 1, we have
Sum of the terms in first bracket = 1 and, (n − 1)^3 + n^3 = (1 − 1)^3 + 1^3 = 1
For n = 2, we have
Sum of the terms in the second bracket = 2 + 3 + 4 = 9 and, (n − 1)^3 + n^3 = (2 − 1)^3 + 2^3 = 1 + 8 = 9
For n = 3, we have
Sum of the terms in the third bracket = (5 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 9) = 35 and, (3 − 1)^3 + 3^3 = 8 + 27 = 35

questions.question: $\frac{3}{4}+\frac{15}{16}+\frac{63}{64}+...$ to n terms is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\frac{3}{4}+\frac{15}{16}+\frac{63}{64}+...{/tex} to n terms is equal to
questions.explanation:
$\frac{3}{4}+\frac{15}{16}+\frac{63}{64}+\ldots .$ to n terms
= $\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)+\left(1-\frac{1}{16}\right)+\left(1-\frac{1}{64}\right)$ + .... to n terms
= n - $\frac{1}{4}\left(1+\frac{1}{4}\right.\left.+\frac{1}{4^{2}}+\ldots . . \text { to } \mathrm{n} \text { terms }\right) $ 
= $n-\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{1-\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{n}}{1-\frac{1}{4}}$ 
= $n-\frac{1}{3}\left(1-4^{-n}\right)$

questions.question: $\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{2}}{1^3}+\frac{\frac{2}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{2}}{1^3+2^3}+\frac{\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{4}{2}}{1^3+2^3+3^3}+\ldots$ upto n terms is equal to

questions.explanation: Replace n = 1 we have $\frac{ 1}{2}$. When n = 2 we have $\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{2}{3}$, (by the principle of mathematical induction when n = 1 we need to take one term from LHS. and for n = 2 we need to take the sum of two terms from the LHS. ....)

questions.question: $\frac{1}{3.5}+\frac{1}{5.7}+\frac{1}{7.9}+\ldots$ up to n terms is equal to
questions.question_raw: {tex}\frac{1}{3.5}+\frac{1}{5.7}+\frac{1}{7.9}+\ldots{/tex} up to n terms is equal to
questions.explanation:
By the process of mathematical induction when n = 1 we have $\frac{1}{15} $.
When n = 2 we have LHS : $\frac{1}{15} + \frac{1}{35} = \frac{2}{21}$, RHS :$\frac{2}{(3( 4+3))}= \frac{2}{21}$, which is true.

questions.question: For all positive integers n, the number 4^n + 15n - 1 is divisible by :
questions.question_raw: For all positive integers n, the number 4n + 15n - 1 is divisible by :
questions.explanation: Replace n = 1 we get 18 n = 2 we get 45.... By the principle of mathematical induction it is divisible by 9.

questions.question: For all $n \in N$, 7^2n - 48n - 1 is divisible by :
questions.question_raw: For all {tex}n \in N{/tex}, 72n - 48n - 1 is divisible by :
questions.explanation: When n = 1 the value is 0. When n = 2 the value is 2304..... Hence by the principle of mathematical induction the expression is divisible by 2304.

questions.question: For all $n \in N$ 5^2n - 1 is divisible by :
questions.question_raw: For all {tex}n \in N{/tex} 52n - 1 is divisible by :
questions.explanation: Replace n = 1, 2, 3.. the answers are divisible by 24.

questions.question: For all $n \in N$, 3^2n + 7 is divisible by :
questions.question_raw: For all {tex}n \in N{/tex}, 32n + 7 is divisible by :
questions.explanation: When n = 1 the value is 16 which is a multple of 8, n = 2 the value is 88 which is a multiple of 8..... By PMI the expression is divisible by 8 for all natural numbers.

questions.question: For all $n \in N$, 3n^5 + 5n^3 + 7n is divisible by :
questions.question_raw: For all {tex}n \in N{/tex}, 3n5 + 5n3 + 7n is divisible by :
questions.explanation: For n = 1 the value is 15. For n = 2 the value is 150 which is a multiple of 15... By PMI the expression is divisible by 15.

questions.question: The nth terms of the series 3 + 7 + 13 + 21 +………. is =
questions.question_raw: The nth terms of the series 3 + 7 + 13 + 21 +………. is =
questions.explanation: Put n = 1 we have 3. When n = 2, 4 + 2 + 1 = 7...

questions.question: The nth terms of the series 4 + 14 + 30 + 52 + 80 + 114 + ….. is =
questions.question_raw: The nth terms of the series 4 + 14 + 30 + 52 + 80 + 114 + ….. is =
questions.explanation: When n = 1 we get 4. When n = 2 we get 12 + 2 = 14...

questions.question: 3 + 13 + 29 + 51 + 79 +……….. to n terms is
questions.question_raw: 3 + 13 + 29 + 51 + 79 +……….. to n terms is
questions.explanation:
When n = 1 we have 3 when n = 2 we have
LHS : 3 + 13 = 16 
RHS : 8 + 8 = 16 ..... By PMI this is the sum of the n terms of the series.

questions.question: The sum of n terms of the series 1^3 + 3^3 + 5^3 + ...... is
questions.question_raw: The sum of n terms of the series 13 + 33 + 53 + ...... is
questions.explanation:
When n = 1 we get 1. When n = 2 we get 
RHS : 4 $\times$ 7 = 28 
LHS : 1 + 27 = 28...... By PMI this is true.

questions.question: If n is an odd positive integer, then a^n + b^n is divisible by:
questions.question_raw: If n is an odd positive integer, then an + bn is divisible by:
questions.explanation: Since when n = 1 we get a + b. Hence by the method of mathematical induction, it is true.

questions.question: If n is an even positive integer, a^n + b^n is divisible by :
questions.question_raw: If n is an even positive integer, an + bn is divisible by :
questions.explanation: If n is even we get a^2 + b^2 which is not divisible by a + b. same case when n = 4.

questions.question: For all $n \in N, \frac{n^5}{5}+\frac{n^3}{3}+\frac{7n}{15 }$ is
questions.question_raw: For all {tex}n \in N, \frac{n^5}{5}+\frac{n^3}{3}+\frac{7n}{15 }{/tex} is
questions.explanation: a natural number

questions.question: The sum of n terms of the series 1 + (1 + a) + (1 + a + a^2) + (1 + a + a^2 + a^3)....., is
questions.question_raw: The sum of n terms of the series 1 + (1 + a) + (1 + a + a2) + (1 + a + a2 + a3)....., is
questions.explanation: Replace n = 1 we get 1. Replace n = 2 we get LHS; a + 2 RHS : a + 2.

questions.question: $\frac{(n+2) !}{(n-1) !}$ is divisible by:
questions.question_raw: {tex}\frac{(n+2) !}{(n-1) !}{/tex} is divisible by:
questions.explanation: When n = 1 we have 3! = 6 and n = 2 we have 4! = 24 which is divisible by 6.
